topics.title: Sorting Algorithms
topics.story: Sorting is the single most-studied problem in computer science. From bubble sort invented in the 1950s to TimSort (Python/Java's default since 2002) — the journey shows how algorithmic thinking evolves. In interviews, you rarely need to implement a sort from scratch, but knowing why merge sort is O(n log n), when counting sort beats it, and what makes quicksort fast in practice separates A from B candidates.
topics.analogy: Merge sort: split a deck of cards in half, sort each half separately, merge them in order — like two sorted hands merging into one. Quick sort: pick a pivot card, put smaller cards left and larger right, repeat for each group.
topics.memoryAnchor: O(n log n) comparison sorts: Merge (stable, O(n) space), Quick (O(log n) space, unstable), Heap (in-place, unstable). O(n) non-comparison: Counting (bounded range), Radix (digit by digit), Bucket (uniform distribution).
topics.firstPrinciples: Lower bound for comparison-based sorting: Ω(n log n) (information-theoretic). Non-comparison sorts break this bound by exploiting structure in keys. Stability matters when secondary sort order must be preserved. In-place means O(1) extra space (ignoring recursion stack).
topics.bruteForce: Bubble sort / Selection sort / Insertion sort: O(n²) time, simple to implement. Insertion sort is O(n) on nearly-sorted input — this is why TimSort uses it for small runs.
topics.optimizedApproach: For general sorting: use Java's Arrays.sort() (dual-pivot quicksort for primitives, TimSort for objects). For counting occurrences in bounded range: counting sort. For sorting by multiple criteria: stable sort (merge/TimSort). For nearly-sorted data: insertion sort.
topics.whenToUse: Problem asks to 'sort and then...': interval merging, meeting rooms, H-index. Custom comparator needed: sort by frequency, sort by multiple keys. Partial sort needed (top-K): heap sort. Integer keys in known range: counting sort O(n+k).

examples.title: Merge Sort
examples.explanation: Divide: split array at mid. Conquer: recursively sort both halves. Combine: merge sorted halves. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n) by Master Theorem.

problems.title: Sort an Array (Implement Merge Sort)
problems.description:
Given an array of integers nums, sort the array in ascending order and return it. Implement merge sort (O(n log n) time, O(n) space).

**Example:**
Input: [5,2,3,1] → Output: [1,2,3,5]
Input: [5,1,1,2,0,0] → Output: [0,0,1,1,2,5]
problems.hint1: Merge sort has two phases. What are they? When does the recursion stop?
problems.hint2: Phase 1: recursively sort left half (arr[left..mid]) and right half (arr[mid+1..right]). Base case: left >= right (single element is sorted).
problems.hint3: Phase 2 (merge): use a temp array. Two pointers, one in each sorted half. Always pick the smaller element. Copy remaining elements.
problems.editorial: Merge sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) space (temp arrays during merge). Stable sort — equal elements maintain relative order. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n) by Master Theorem (case 2: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n).

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.description:
Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [start, end], merge all overlapping intervals and return the non-overlapping result.

**Example:**
Input: [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]] → Output: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
Input: [[1,4],[4,5]] → Output: [[1,5]]
problems.hint1: If intervals are sorted by start time, which intervals can overlap with the current one?
problems.hint2: Sort by start. Iterate: if current interval starts before the previous one ends, merge them (extend end to max). Otherwise, start a new group.
problems.hint3: Track the last merged interval. If intervals[i][0] <= lastEnd, update lastEnd = max(lastEnd, intervals[i][1]). Otherwise push the last interval and start a new one.
problems.editorial: Sort by start: O(n log n). Single pass merge: O(n). Overlap condition: next.start <= current.end. Merge by extending current.end to max(current.end, next.end). Total: O(n log n).

problems.title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums, return an array counts where counts[i] is the number of smaller elements to the right of nums[i].

**Example:**
Input: [5,2,6,1] → Output: [2,1,1,0]
(5 has 2 smaller to right: 2,1. 2 has 1: 1. 6 has 1: 1. 1 has 0.)
problems.hint1: Brute force is O(n²). Can the merge step of merge sort count inversions?
problems.hint2: During the merge phase, when you pick an element from the RIGHT half, all remaining elements in the LEFT half are larger than it — that counts are inversions.
problems.hint3: Modified merge sort: track original indices. When right[j] goes before left[i], add (left.remaining count) to counts[original index of right[j]].
problems.editorial: Modified merge sort tracking original indices. During merge, when a right-side element is chosen over left-side elements, ALL remaining left-side elements are larger → increment their counts. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Meeting Rooms II
problems.description:
Given an array of meeting time intervals [start, end], find the minimum number of conference rooms required.

**Example:**
Input: [[0,30],[5,10],[15,20]] → Output: 2
Input: [[7,10],[2,4]] → Output: 1
problems.hint1: Sort meetings by start time. For each meeting, can it use an existing free room?
problems.hint2: Use a min-heap of meeting end times. For each new meeting, if heap.peek() <= meeting.start, the earliest-ending room is free — reuse it. Otherwise open a new room.
problems.hint3: Heap size at the end = minimum rooms needed. The heap tracks when each room's current meeting ends.
problems.editorial: Greedy with min-heap of end times: sort by start (O(n log n)), then process each meeting. If earliest-ending room is free (peek <= current start), reuse it. Heap size = rooms in use = answer. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Largest Number
problems.description:
Given a list of non-negative integers nums, arrange them such that they form the largest number and return it as a string.

**Example:**
Input: [10,2] → Output: '210'
Input: [3,30,34,5,9] → Output: '9534330'
problems.hint1: How do you decide which of two numbers should come first? Is 9 > 30, so 930 > 309 — yes. Is 3 > 30? 330 vs 303 → 330, so 3 > 30 in this ordering.
problems.hint2: Custom comparator: compare a+b vs b+a as strings. If a+b > b+a, place a first.
problems.hint3: Sort with the custom comparator, join. Edge case: if the largest number is 0 (all zeros), return '0'.
problems.editorial: Custom comparator: (a, b) → compare strings (b+a) vs (a+b). This creates a total ordering consistent with which concatenation is larger. Proof: this comparator is transitive — if a>b and b>c then a>c in this ordering. O(n log n * L) where L = average number length.

problems.title: Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)
problems.description:
Given an array nums with only 0s, 1s, and 2s, sort it in-place in one pass without using sort().

**Example:**
Input: [2,0,2,1,1,0] → Output: [0,0,1,1,2,2]
Input: [2,0,1] → Output: [0,1,2]
problems.hint1: Three-way partitioning. Use three pointers for three zones: [0..low-1] = all 0s, [low..mid-1] = all 1s, [high+1..n-1] = all 2s.
problems.hint2: Initialize low=0, mid=0, high=n-1. If nums[mid]==0: swap with low, advance both low and mid. If nums[mid]==2: swap with high, decrement high (don't advance mid). If nums[mid]==1: just advance mid.
problems.hint3: Stop when mid > high. The key insight: when swapping with low, we know nums[low] is 1 (already processed), so we can advance mid. When swapping with high, nums[high] is unknown, so don't advance mid.
problems.editorial: Dutch National Flag (Dijkstra 1976). O(n) single pass, O(1) space. Three invariants maintained: [0..low-1]=0s, [low..mid-1]=1s, [high+1..n-1]=2s, [mid..high]=unknown. Stop when mid > high (no more unknown region).

problems.title: Sort Characters by Frequency
problems.description:
Given a string s, sort it in decreasing order of character frequency. If multiple valid answers exist, return any.

**Example:**
Input: 'tree' → Output: 'eert' or 'eetr'
Input: 'cccaaa' → Output: 'cccaaa' or 'aaaccc'
Input: 'Aabb' → Output: 'bbAa' or 'bbaA'
problems.hint1: Count frequencies. Then sort by frequency (descending). How do you rebuild the string?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap for frequency count, then a max-heap or sort the entries by count. Repeat each character by its frequency.
problems.hint3: Bucket sort alternative: create buckets[i] = list of chars with frequency i. Iterate from n to 1, appending chars * frequency.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 5*10^5 | s consists of uppercase/lowercase letters and digits
problems.editorial: Count + max-heap: O(n + k log k) where k = unique chars. Bucket sort: O(n) — create array of size n+1, buckets[freq] = list of chars. Iterate from high to low frequency for O(n) total. String.repeat() for clean char multiplication.

problems.title: Sort Linked List (Merge Sort)
problems.description:
Given the head of a linked list, return the list sorted in ascending order. Do it in O(n log n) time and O(1) space.

**Example:**
Input: [4,2,1,3] → Output: [1,2,3,4]
Input: [-1,5,3,4,0] → Output: [-1,0,3,4,5]
problems.hint1: Merge sort on a linked list: split → sort each half → merge. How do you split a linked list in half?
problems.hint2: Find middle using slow/fast pointers. Split at middle (set middle.next = null). Recursively sort both halves. Merge sorted halves using the merge-two-sorted-lists technique.
problems.hint3: Merge step: use a dummy head node. Compare heads of both halves, attach smaller to result, advance that pointer.
problems.editorial: Merge sort on linked list: O(n log n) time, O(log n) recursive stack space. Bottom-up iterative merge sort achieves O(1) space. fast=head.next (not head) ensures slow lands at first middle for even-length lists — allows clean split.

problems.title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
problems.description:
Given an n×n matrix where each row and column is sorted in ascending order, return the kth smallest element.

**Example:**
matrix=[[1,5,9],[10,11,13],[12,13,15]], k=8 → Output: 13
problems.hint1: The matrix's sorted rows suggest a merge-K-sorted approach. Or use binary search on the value range.
problems.hint2: Min-heap: start with first column elements. Poll minimum, push the next element from the same row. Do this k times.
problems.hint3: Binary search alternative: search in the value range [matrix[0][0], matrix[n-1][n-1]]. For each mid, count elements ≤ mid. The kth smallest is the smallest value where count ≥ k.
problems.constraints: n == matrix.length == matrix[i].length | 1 ≤ n ≤ 300 | -10^9 ≤ matrix[i][j] ≤ 10^9 | Each row and column is sorted | 1 ≤ k ≤ n²
problems.editorial: Min-heap approach: O(k log n). Binary search approach: O(n log(max-min)) where count(mid) uses the column-sorted property to count ≤ mid in O(n). Binary search is O(n log(val_range)) and doesn't depend on k.

problems.title: Maximum Gap
problems.description:
Given an unsorted array, find the maximum difference between successive elements in the sorted form. Return 0 if the array contains less than 2 elements.

Solve in O(n) time and space.

**Example:**
Input: [3,6,9,1] → Output: 3 (sorted: [1,3,6,9], max gap = 3)
Input: [10] → Output: 0
problems.hint1: If we sort, max gap is O(n log n). The constraint is O(n). Think about bucket sort / pigeonhole principle.
problems.hint2: Pigeonhole: with n elements in range [min, max], the maximum gap must be ≥ ceil((max-min)/(n-1)). Create n-1 buckets of this size — any gap spanning two non-empty buckets is the answer.
problems.hint3: Each bucket stores only the min and max of its elements. The max gap = max over all adjacent non-empty bucket pairs of (next_bucket.min - prev_bucket.max). Internal bucket gaps are ≤ bucket size and cannot be the maximum gap.
problems.editorial: Bucket sort / pigeonhole: O(n) time and space. Key insight: with n-1 gaps among n sorted elements, at least one gap ≥ (max-min)/(n-1). Buckets of this size guarantee the maximum gap spans bucket boundaries. Store only min/max per bucket — the intra-bucket gap is always ≤ bucket size.

problems.title: Wiggle Sort II
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums, reorder it such that nums[0] < nums[1] > nums[2] < nums[3]... (strict inequalities). A valid arrangement is always guaranteed.

**Example:**
Input: [1,5,1,1,6,4] → Output: [1,6,1,5,1,4] (or [1,4,1,5,1,6])
Input: [1,3,2,2,3,1] → Output: [2,3,1,3,1,2]
problems.hint1: Simple approach: sort the array, then interleave smaller half at even indices and larger half at odd indices. Why does naive interleaving fail?
problems.hint2: After sorting, split into two halves: smaller = nums[0..mid], larger = nums[mid+1..n-1]. Place larger half at odd positions (1,3,5,...) and smaller half at even positions (0,2,4,...). Crucially, fill from right to left to avoid equal element adjacency.
problems.hint3: Sort, find mid. For odd indices (n-1, n-3, ...): fill with larger half (right to left). For even indices (n-2 or n, n-4, ...): fill with smaller half (right to left). This guarantees no equal elements meet at adjacent positions.
problems.editorial: Sort then interleave from the right. Split at mid: smaller half = sorted[0..mid], larger half = sorted[mid+1..n-1]. Place larger half at odd positions (descending from end), smaller half at even positions (descending from mid). Filling right-to-left ensures duplicate elements in the median region don't end up adjacent. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Pancake Sorting
problems.description:
Given an array arr, perform pancake flips to sort it. A pancake flip of size k reverses the first k elements. Return the sequence of k values for each flip performed to sort the array. Any valid answer is accepted (sequence length ≤ 10*n).

**Example:**
Input: [3,2,4,1] → Output: [4,2,4,3] (flip first 4, flip first 2, flip first 4, flip first 3)
problems.hint1: Think about selection sort: to place the largest element in its correct position, you need at most 2 flips. Can you generalize?
problems.hint2: To place the element with value i at position i (1-indexed): first flip it to the top (position 0) with a flip of size = its current index + 1. Then flip it to its correct position with a flip of size i.
problems.hint3: Iterate i from n down to 2. Find the current position of value i (maxIdx). If already at position i-1, skip. Else: if not at position 0, flip(maxIdx+1) to bring it to front. Then flip(i) to send it to position i-1.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"[3,2,4,1]","output":"valid sequence of pancake flips that sorts the array"},{"input":"[1,2,3]","output":"[]"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ arr.length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ arr[i] ≤ arr.length | All integers in arr are unique
problems.editorial: Pancake selection sort: O(n²) time, O(n) output. For each size from n to 2: find max in arr[0..size-1], flip it to front (if needed), flip to position size-1. At most 2 flips per iteration × n iterations = at most 2n flips total (within 10n constraint). Each flip reverses a prefix — simulated with standard two-pointer reverse.
